'''Rebellion of Tachibana no Naramaro''' (橘奈良麻呂の乱, Tachibana no Naramaro no Ran) was a political coup attempt during the [[Nara period]]. In the ninth year of [[Tenpyō-Shōhō]] (757 CE), [[Tachibana no Naramaro]] conspired to overthrow [[Fujiwara no Nakamaro]] and plotted to depose the emperor, but the scheme was discovered through betrayal and ended in failure.
== Summary == === Preceding Events === The father of Tachibana Naramaro, [[Left Minister|{{ill|Sadaijin|ja|左大臣}}]] {{ill|Tachibana no Moroe|ja|橘諸兄}}, held political power during the reign of {{ill|Emperor Shōmū|ja|聖武天皇}}.
In [[743]] ({{ill|Tenpyō|ja|天平}} 15), during Emperor Shōmū’s illness while staying in Naniwa, Naramaro revealed plans to {{ill|Saeki Zenjō|ja|佐伯全成}}, conspiring with {{ill|Ono no Azumabito|ja|小野東人}} and others to support {{ill|Prince Ōbu|ja|黄文王}} as the next emperor. Although Princess {{ill|Abe no Naishinnō|ja|阿倍内親王}} had already been appointed crown princess in [[738]] (Tenpyō 10), Naramaro claimed there was no established successor, as female emperors were single (either unmarried or widowed) and limited to a single reign. The move reflected an underlying desire for a male heir.
In [[749]] (Tenpyō 21/Tenpyō Kanpō 1/Tenpyō Shōhō 1), Emperor Shōmū abdicated, and Abe no Naishinnō ascended as {{ill|Empress Kōken|ja|孝謙天皇}}. {{ill|Empress Dowager Kōmyō|ja|光明皇后}} trusted {{ill|Fujiwara no Nakamaro|ja|藤原仲麻呂}}, appointing him head of the newly established {{ill|Shibi Chūdai|ja|紫微中台}}. Nakamaro, favored by Empress Kōken, quickly rose in power, while Naramaro’s family influence waned. In November, during the enthronement {{ill|Daijōsai|ja|大嘗祭}}, Naramaro again conspired with Saeki Zenjō but failed to execute the plan due to Zenjō’s refusal.
In [[755]] (Tenpyō Shōhō 7), {{ill|Sami no Miyamori|ja|佐味宮守}} accused Moroe of criticizing the court during a feast. Although Emperor Shōmū dismissed the issue, {{ill|Saeki Minomaro|ja|佐伯美濃麻呂}} was questioned as a witness. When Minomaro redirected attention to Zenjō, the matter ended with Empress Dowager Kōmyō’s intervention.
In February [[756]] (Tenpyō Shōhō 8), Moroe resigned, humiliated. In April, during Emperor Shōmū’s final illness, Naramaro attempted to conspire once again, involving {{ill|Ōtomo no Komaro|ja|大伴古麻呂}} but failed. On May 2, Emperor Shōmū passed away, and {{ill|Prince Funado|ja|道祖王}} was named crown prince per his will.
In January [[757]] (Tenpyō Shōhō 9), Moroe died at 74. Scholars like {{ill|Morita Tei|ja|森田悌}} and {{ill|Nakamura Junshō|ja|中村順昭}} suggest Moroe opposed Naramaro’s plans or was unaware of them, while {{ill|Kimoto Yoshinobu|ja|木本好信}} argues Moroe may have been indirectly involved.
In March, Prince Funado fell out of favor and was deposed. In April, deliberations on the next crown prince led to {{ill|Prince Ōtsu|ja|塩焼王}} being rejected. Instead, {{ill|Prince Ōī|ja|大炊王}} (later {{ill|Emperor Junnin|ja|淳仁天皇}}), supported by Nakamaro, was chosen.
=== The Conspiracy and Its Discovery === Dissatisfied with Nakamaro’s dominance, Naramaro gathered like-minded individuals to plot his removal{{Efn|Nakamaro, aware of Naramaro’s movements, preemptively transferred {{ill|Ōtomo no Komaro|ja|大伴古麻呂}} to Mutsu Province on June 16.}}. On {{ill|June 28|6月28日 (k久治光平)}} (July 18), {{ill|Prince Yamashiro|ja|山背王}} reported the conspiracy to Empress Kōken. The next day, a meeting was held where Naramaro and 20 others, including Komaro, {{ill|Prince Ōbu|ja|黄文王}}, and {{ill|Prince Anjuku|ja|安宿王}}, swore to rebel on {{ill|July 2|7月2日 (k久治光平)}} (July 22).
On July 2, Empress Kōken and Empress Dowager Kōmyō issued an edict urging loyalty. However, that evening, {{ill|Uemichi no Fiyata|ja|上道斐太都}} exposed the plot to Nakamaro. By the next day, Naramaro, Komaro, and others were arrested. Torture extracted confessions of plans to attack Nakamaro’s residence, depose the crown prince, and install a new emperor.
=== Harsh Punishments === On {{ill|July 4|7月4日 (k久治光平)}} (July 24), suspects, including Prince Funado, Prince Ōbu, and Naramaro, were tortured. Many, including Naramaro, died under interrogation. Prince Anjuku was exiled to Sado Island, and others faced demotions or exile. Over 443 individuals were punished.
=== Aftermath === Nakamaro used the incident to consolidate power. In [[758]] (Tenpyō Hōji 2), Prince Ōī ascended as Emperor Junnin, elevating Nakamaro to {{ill|Taishi|ja|太師}} (Grand Minister). However, Nakamaro’s fall came swiftly. By [[764]] (Tenpyō Hōji 8), he rebelled and was defeated, marking the end of his dominance.
== References == * {{Cite book|author=Shigeru Kitayama|title=Tachibana no Naramaro Rebellion|publisher=Ritsumeikan Law Journal|year=1952}} * {{Cite book|author=Yukihiro Nakagawa|title=Ancient Japanese History: Man'yō People|publisher=Kasama Publishing|year=1975}} * {{Cite book|author=Katsumi Kikuchi|title=The Rebellion of Tachibana no Naramaro|publisher=Historical Reader|year=1998}} * {{Cite book|author=Yoshinobu Kimoto|title=Nara Period Figures and Conflicts|publisher=Ofu|year=2003}} * {{Cite book|author=Masanori Ueno|title=The Inner Politics of the Nara Court|publisher=Yagi Books|year=2023}}

In March, Prince Funado fell out of favor and was deposed. In April, deliberations on the next crown prince led to {{ill|Prince Ōtsu|ja|塩焼王}} being rejected. Instead, {{ill|Prince Ōī|ja|大炊王}} (later {{ill|Emperor Junnin|ja|淳仁天皇}}), supported by Nakamaro, was chosen.
=== The Conspiracy and Its Discovery === Dissatisfied with Nakamaro’s dominance, Naramaro gathered like-minded individuals to plot his removal{{Efn|Nakamaro, aware of Naramaro’s movements, preemptively transferred {{ill|Ōtomo no Komaro|ja|大伴古麻呂}} to Mutsu Province on June 16.}}. On {{ill|June 28|6月28日 (k久治光平)}} (July 18), {{ill|Prince Yamashiro|ja|山背王}} reported the conspiracy to Empress Kōken. The next day, a meeting was held where Naramaro and 20 others, including Komaro, {{ill|Prince Ōbu|ja|黄文王}}, and {{ill|Prince Anjuku|ja|安宿王}}, swore to rebel on {{ill|July 2|7月2日 (k久治光平)}} (July 22).
On July 2, Empress Kōken and Empress Dowager Kōmyō issued an edict urging loyalty. However, that evening, {{ill|Uemichi no Fiyata|ja|上道斐太都}} exposed the plot to Nakamaro. By the next day, Naramaro, Komaro, and others were arrested. Torture extracted confessions of plans to attack Nakamaro’s residence, depose the crown prince, and install a new emperor.
=== Harsh Punishments === On {{ill|July 4|7月4日 (k久治光平)}} (July 24), suspects, including Prince Funado, Prince Ōbu, and Naramaro, were tortured. Many, including Naramaro, died under interrogation. Prince Anjuku was exiled to Sado Island, and others faced demotions or exile. Over 443 individuals were punished.
=== Aftermath === Nakamaro used the incident to consolidate power. In [[758]] (Tenpyō Hōji 2), Prince Ōī ascended as Emperor Junnin, elevating Nakamaro to {{ill|Taishi|ja|太師}} (Grand Minister). However, Nakamaro’s fall came swiftly. By [[764]] (Tenpyō Hōji 8), he rebelled and was defeated, marking the end of his dominance.

'''Rebellion of Tachibana no Naramaro''' (橘奈良麻呂の乱, Tachibana no Naramaro no Ran) was a political coup attempt during the [[Nara period]]. In the ninth year of [[Tenpyō-Shōhō]] (757 CE), [[Tachibana no Naramaro]] conspired to overthrow [[Fujiwara no Nakamaro]] and plotted to depose the emperor, but the scheme was discovered through betrayal and ended in failure.
== Summary == === Preceding Events === The father of Tachibana Naramaro, [[Left Minister|{{ill|Sadaijin|ja|左大臣}}]] {{ill|Tachibana no Moroe|ja|橘諸兄}}, held political power during the reign of {{ill|Emperor Shōmū|ja|聖武天皇}}.
In [[743]] ({{ill|Tenpyō|ja|天平}} 15), during Emperor Shōmū’s illness while staying in Naniwa, Naramaro revealed plans to {{ill|Saeki Zenjō|ja|佐伯全成}}, conspiring with {{ill|Ono no Azumabito|ja|小野東人}} and others to support {{ill|Prince Ōbu|ja|黄文王}} as the next emperor. Although Princess {{ill|Abe no Naishinnō|ja|阿倍内親王}} had already been appointed crown princess in [[738]] (Tenpyō 10), Naramaro claimed there was no established successor, as female emperors were single (either unmarried or widowed) and limited to a single reign. The move reflected an underlying desire for a male heir.
In [[749]] (Tenpyō 21/Tenpyō Kanpō 1/Tenpyō Shōhō 1), Emperor Shōmū abdicated, and Abe no Naishinnō ascended as {{ill|Empress Kōken|ja|孝謙天皇}}. {{ill|Empress Dowager Kōmyō|ja|光明皇后}} trusted {{ill|Fujiwara no Nakamaro|ja|藤原仲麻呂}}, appointing him head of the newly established {{ill|Shibi Chūdai|ja|紫微中台}}. Nakamaro, favored by Empress Kōken, quickly rose in power, while Naramaro’s family influence waned. In November, during the enthronement {{ill|Daijōsai|ja|大嘗祭}}, Naramaro again conspired with Saeki Zenjō but failed to execute the plan due to Zenjō’s refusal.
In [[755]] (Tenpyō Shōhō 7), {{ill|Sami no Miyamori|ja|佐味宮守}} accused Moroe of criticizing the court during a feast. Although Emperor Shōmū dismissed the issue, {{ill|Saeki Minomaro|ja|佐伯美濃麻呂}} was questioned as a witness. When Minomaro redirected attention to Zenjō, the matter ended with Empress Dowager Kōmyō’s intervention.
In February [[756]] (Tenpyō Shōhō 8), Moroe resigned, humiliated. In April, during Emperor Shōmū’s final illness, Naramaro attempted to conspire once again, involving {{ill|Ōtomo no Komaro|ja|大伴古麻呂}} but failed. On May 2, Emperor Shōmū passed away, and {{ill|Prince Funado|ja|道祖王}} was named crown prince per his will.
In January [[757]] (Tenpyō Shōhō 9), Moroe died at 74. Scholars like {{ill|Morita Tei|ja|森田悌}} and {{ill|Nakamura Junshō|ja|中村順昭}} suggest Moroe opposed Naramaro’s plans or was unaware of them, while {{ill|Kimoto Yoshinobu|ja|木本好信}} argues Moroe may have been indirectly involved.
In March, Prince Funado fell out of favor and was deposed. In April, deliberations on the next crown prince led to {{ill|Prince Ōtsu|ja|塩焼王}} being rejected. Instead, {{ill|Prince Ōī|ja|大炊王}} (later {{ill|Emperor Junnin|ja|淳仁天皇}}), supported by Nakamaro, was chosen.
=== The Conspiracy and Its Discovery === Dissatisfied with Nakamaro’s dominance, Naramaro gathered like-minded individuals to plot his removal{{Efn|Nakamaro, aware of Naramaro’s movements, preemptively transferred {{ill|Ōtomo no Komaro|ja|大伴古麻呂}} to Mutsu Province on June 16.}}. On {{ill|June 28|6月28日 (k久治光平)}} (July 18), {{ill|Prince Yamashiro|ja|山背王}} reported the conspiracy to Empress Kōken. The next day, a meeting was held where Naramaro and 20 others, including Komaro, {{ill|Prince Ōbu|ja|黄文王}}, and {{ill|Prince Anjuku|ja|安宿王}}, swore to rebel on {{ill|July 2|7月2日 (k久治光平)}} (July 22).
On July 2, Empress Kōken and Empress Dowager Kōmyō issued an edict urging loyalty. However, that evening, {{ill|Uemichi no Fiyata|ja|上道斐太都}} exposed the plot to Nakamaro. By the next day, Naramaro, Komaro, and others were arrested. Torture extracted confessions of plans to attack Nakamaro’s residence, depose the crown prince, and install a new emperor.
=== Harsh Punishments === On {{ill|July 4|7月4日 (k久治光平)}} (July 24), suspects, including Prince Funado, Prince Ōbu, and Naramaro, were tortured. Many, including Naramaro, died under interrogation. Prince Anjuku was exiled to Sado Island, and others faced demotions or exile. Over 443 individuals were punished.
=== Aftermath === Nakamaro used the incident to consolidate power. In [[758]] (Tenpyō Hōji 2), Prince Ōī ascended as Emperor Junnin, elevating Nakamaro to {{ill|Taishi|ja|太師}} (Grand Minister). However, Nakamaro’s fall came swiftly. By [[764]] (Tenpyō Hōji 8), he rebelled and was defeated, marking the end of his dominance.

[[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-Tachibana no Naramaro Rebellion-qq]] [[Category:Tachibana no Naramaro Rebellion]][[category:Tachibana no Naramaro Rebellion]] [[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-Tachibana no Naramaro Rebellion-qq]] [[Category:Tachibana no Naramaro Rebellion]][[Category:pp]][[category:Tachibana no Naramaro Rebellion]][[Category:pp]] [[Category:attempting to force efresh]] [[Category:attempting to force refresh]] [[Category:bump 1-25]] [[Category:q--q]] [[Category:q--q]] [[Category:subst:MAINSPACE]][[sn:Tachibana no Naramaro Rebellion]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Tachibana no Naramaro Rebellion]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Tachibana no Naramaro Rebellion]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Tachibana no Naramaro Rebellion]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:subst:MAINSPACE]][[sn:Tachibana no Naramaro Rebellion]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Tachibana no Naramaro Rebellion]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Tachibana no Naramaro Rebellion]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Tachibana no Naramaro Rebellion]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Tachibana no Naramaro Rebellion]][[Category:qq]] [[ja:橘奈良麻呂の乱]]

'''Rebellion of Tachibana no Naramaro''' (橘奈良麻呂の乱, Tachibana no Naramaro no Ran) was a political coup attempt during the [[Nara period]]. In the ninth year of [[Tenpyō-Shōhō]] (757 CE), [[Tachibana no Naramaro]] conspired to overthrow [[Fujiwara no Nakamaro]] and plotted to depose the emperor, but the scheme was discovered through betrayal and ended in failure.
== Summary == === Preceding Events === The father of Tachibana Naramaro, [[Left Minister|{{ill|Sadaijin|ja|左大臣}}]] {{ill|Tachibana no Moroe|ja|橘諸兄}}, held political power during the reign of {{ill|Emperor Shōmū|ja|聖武天皇}}.
In [[743]] ({{ill|Tenpyō|ja|天平}} 15), during Emperor Shōmū’s illness while staying in Naniwa, Naramaro revealed plans to {{ill|Saeki Zenjō|ja|佐伯全成}}, conspiring with {{ill|Ono no Azumabito|ja|小野東人}} and others to support {{ill|Prince Ōbu|ja|黄文王}} as the next emperor. Although Princess {{ill|Abe no Naishinnō|ja|阿倍内親王}} had already been appointed crown princess in [[738]] (Tenpyō 10), Naramaro claimed there was no established successor, as female emperors were single (either unmarried or widowed) and limited to a single reign. The move reflected an underlying desire for a male heir.
In [[749]] (Tenpyō 21/Tenpyō Kanpō 1/Tenpyō Shōhō 1), Emperor Shōmū abdicated, and Abe no Naishinnō ascended as {{ill|Empress Kōken|ja|孝謙天皇}}. {{ill|Empress Dowager Kōmyō|ja|光明皇后}} trusted {{ill|Fujiwara no Nakamaro|ja|藤原仲麻呂}}, appointing him head of the newly established {{ill|Shibi Chūdai|ja|紫微中台}}. Nakamaro, favored by Empress Kōken, quickly rose in power, while Naramaro’s family influence waned. In November, during the enthronement {{ill|Daijōsai|ja|大嘗祭}}, Naramaro again conspired with Saeki Zenjō but failed to execute the plan due to Zenjō’s refusal.
In [[755]] (Tenpyō Shōhō 7), {{ill|Sami no Miyamori|ja|佐味宮守}} accused Moroe of criticizing the court during a feast. Although Emperor Shōmū dismissed the issue, {{ill|Saeki Minomaro|ja|佐伯美濃麻呂}} was questioned as a witness. When Minomaro redirected attention to Zenjō, the matter ended with Empress Dowager Kōmyō’s intervention.
In February [[756]] (Tenpyō Shōhō 8), Moroe resigned, humiliated. In April, during Emperor Shōmū’s final illness, Naramaro attempted to conspire once again, involving {{ill|Ōtomo no Komaro|ja|大伴古麻呂}} but failed. On May 2, Emperor Shōmū passed away, and {{ill|Prince Funado|ja|道祖王}} was named crown prince per his will.
In January [[757]] (Tenpyō Shōhō 9), Moroe died at 74. Scholars like {{ill|Morita Tei|ja|森田悌}} and {{ill|Nakamura Junshō|ja|中村順昭}} suggest Moroe opposed Naramaro’s plans or was unaware of them, while {{ill|Kimoto Yoshinobu|ja|木本好信}} argues Moroe may have been indirectly involved.
In March, Prince Funado fell out of favor and was deposed. In April, deliberations on the next crown prince led to {{ill|Prince Ōtsu|ja|塩焼王}} being rejected. Instead, {{ill|Prince Ōī|ja|大炊王}} (later {{ill|Emperor Junnin|ja|淳仁天皇}}), supported by Nakamaro, was chosen.
=== The Conspiracy and Its Discovery === Dissatisfied with Nakamaro’s dominance, Naramaro gathered like-minded individuals to plot his removal{{Efn|Nakamaro, aware of Naramaro’s movements, preemptively transferred {{ill|Ōtomo no Komaro|ja|大伴古麻呂}} to Mutsu Province on June 16.}}. On {{ill|June 28|6月28日 (k久治光平)}} (July 18), {{ill|Prince Yamashiro|ja|山背王}} reported the conspiracy to Empress Kōken. The next day, a meeting was held where Naramaro and 20 others, including Komaro, {{ill|Prince Ōbu|ja|黄文王}}, and {{ill|Prince Anjuku|ja|安宿王}}, swore to rebel on {{ill|July 2|7月2日 (k久治光平)}} (July 22).
On July 2, Empress Kōken and Empress Dowager Kōmyō issued an edict urging loyalty. However, that evening, {{ill|Uemichi no Fiyata|ja|上道斐太都}} exposed the plot to Nakamaro. By the next day, Naramaro, Komaro, and others were arrested. Torture extracted confessions of plans to attack Nakamaro’s residence, depose the crown prince, and install a new emperor.
=== Harsh Punishments === On {{ill|July 4|7月4日 (k久治光平)}} (July 24), suspects, including Prince Funado, Prince Ōbu, and Naramaro, were tortured. Many, including Naramaro, died under interrogation. Prince Anjuku was exiled to Sado Island, and others faced demotions or exile. Over 443 individuals were punished.
=== Aftermath === Nakamaro used the incident to consolidate power. In [[758]] (Tenpyō Hōji 2), Prince Ōī ascended as Emperor Junnin, elevating Nakamaro to {{ill|Taishi|ja|太師}} (Grand Minister). However, Nakamaro’s fall came swiftly. By [[764]] (Tenpyō Hōji 8), he rebelled and was defeated, marking the end of his dominance.
== References == * {{Cite book|author=Shigeru Kitayama|title=Tachibana no Naramaro Rebellion|publisher=Ritsumeikan Law Journal|year=1952}} * {{Cite book|author=Yukihiro Nakagawa|title=Ancient Japanese History: Man'yō People|publisher=Kasama Publishing|year=1975}} * {{Cite book|author=Katsumi Kikuchi|title=The Rebellion of Tachibana no Naramaro|publisher=Historical Reader|year=1998}} * {{Cite book|author=Yoshinobu Kimoto|title=Nara Period Figures and Conflicts|publisher=Ofu|year=2003}} * {{Cite book|author=Masanori Ueno|title=The Inner Politics of the Nara Court|publisher=Yagi Books|year=2023}}

'''Rebellion of Tachibana no Naramaro''' (橘奈良麻呂の乱, Tachibana no Naramaro no Ran) was a political coup attempt during the [[Nara period]]. In the ninth year of [[Tenpyō-Shōhō]] (757 CE), [[Tachibana no Naramaro]] conspired to overthrow [[Fujiwara no Nakamaro]] and plotted to depose the emperor, but the scheme was discovered through betrayal and ended in failure.
== Summary == === Preceding Events === The father of Tachibana Naramaro, [[Left Minister|{{ill|Sadaijin|ja|左大臣}}]] {{ill|Tachibana no Moroe|ja|橘諸兄}}, held political power during the reign of {{ill|Emperor Shōmū|ja|聖武天皇}}.
In [[743]] ({{ill|Tenpyō|ja|天平}} 15), during Emperor Shōmū’s illness while staying in Naniwa, Naramaro revealed plans to {{ill|Saeki Zenjō|ja|佐伯全成}}, conspiring with {{ill|Ono no Azumabito|ja|小野東人}} and others to support {{ill|Prince Ōbu|ja|黄文王}} as the next emperor. Although Princess {{ill|Abe no Naishinnō|ja|阿倍内親王}} had already been appointed crown princess in [[738]] (Tenpyō 10), Naramaro claimed there was no established successor, as female emperors were single (either unmarried or widowed) and limited to a single reign. The move reflected an underlying desire for a male heir.
In [[749]] (Tenpyō 21/Tenpyō Kanpō 1/Tenpyō Shōhō 1), Emperor Shōmū abdicated, and Abe no Naishinnō ascended as {{ill|Empress Kōken|ja|孝謙天皇}}. {{ill|Empress Dowager Kōmyō|ja|光明皇后}} trusted {{ill|Fujiwara no Nakamaro|ja|藤原仲麻呂}}, appointing him head of the newly established {{ill|Shibi Chūdai|ja|紫微中台}}. Nakamaro, favored by Empress Kōken, quickly rose in power, while Naramaro’s family influence waned. In November, during the enthronement {{ill|Daijōsai|ja|大嘗祭}}, Naramaro again conspired with Saeki Zenjō but failed to execute the plan due to Zenjō’s refusal.
In [[755]] (Tenpyō Shōhō 7), {{ill|Sami no Miyamori|ja|佐味宮守}} accused Moroe of criticizing the court during a feast. Although Emperor Shōmū dismissed the issue, {{ill|Saeki Minomaro|ja|佐伯美濃麻呂}} was questioned as a witness. When Minomaro redirected attention to Zenjō, the matter ended with Empress Dowager Kōmyō’s intervention.
In February [[756]] (Tenpyō Shōhō 8), Moroe resigned, humiliated. In April, during Emperor Shōmū’s final illness, Naramaro attempted to conspire once again, involving {{ill|Ōtomo no Komaro|ja|大伴古麻呂}} but failed. On May 2, Emperor Shōmū passed away, and {{ill|Prince Funado|ja|道祖王}} was named crown prince per his will.
In January [[757]] (Tenpyō Shōhō 9), Moroe died at 74. Scholars like {{ill|Morita Tei|ja|森田悌}} and {{ill|Nakamura Junshō|ja|中村順昭}} suggest Moroe opposed Naramaro’s plans or was unaware of them, while {{ill|Kimoto Yoshinobu|ja|木本好信}} argues Moroe may have been indirectly involved.
In March, Prince Funado fell out of favor and was deposed. In April, deliberations on the next crown prince led to {{ill|Prince Ōtsu|ja|塩焼王}} being rejected. Instead, {{ill|Prince Ōī|ja|大炊王}} (later {{ill|Emperor Junnin|ja|淳仁天皇}}), supported by Nakamaro, was chosen.
=== The Conspiracy and Its Discovery === Dissatisfied with Nakamaro’s dominance, Naramaro gathered like-minded individuals to plot his removal{{Efn|Nakamaro, aware of Naramaro’s movements, preemptively transferred {{ill|Ōtomo no Komaro|ja|大伴古麻呂}} to Mutsu Province on June 16.}}. On {{ill|June 28|6月28日 (k久治光平)}} (July 18), {{ill|Prince Yamashiro|ja|山背王}} reported the conspiracy to Empress Kōken. The next day, a meeting was held where Naramaro and 20 others, including Komaro, {{ill|Prince Ōbu|ja|黄文王}}, and {{ill|Prince Anjuku|ja|安宿王}}, swore to rebel on {{ill|July 2|7月2日 (k久治光平)}} (July 22).
On July 2, Empress Kōken and Empress Dowager Kōmyō issued an edict urging loyalty. However, that evening, {{ill|Uemichi no Fiyata|ja|上道斐太都}} exposed the plot to Nakamaro. By the next day, Naramaro, Komaro, and others were arrested. Torture extracted confessions of plans to attack Nakamaro’s residence, depose the crown prince, and install a new emperor.
=== Harsh Punishments === On {{ill|July 4|7月4日 (k久治光平)}} (July 24), suspects, including Prince Funado, Prince Ōbu, and Naramaro, were tortured. Many, including Naramaro, died under interrogation. Prince Anjuku was exiled to Sado Island, and others faced demotions or exile. Over 443 individuals were punished.
=== Aftermath === Nakamaro used the incident to consolidate power. In [[758]] (Tenpyō Hōji 2), Prince Ōī ascended as Emperor Junnin, elevating Nakamaro to {{ill|Taishi|ja|太師}} (Grand Minister). However, Nakamaro’s fall came swiftly. By [[764]] (Tenpyō Hōji 8), he rebelled and was defeated, marking the end of his dominance.

[[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-Tachibana no Naramaro Rebellion-qq]] [[Category:Tachibana no Naramaro Rebellion]][[category:Tachibana no Naramaro Rebellion]] [[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-Tachibana no Naramaro Rebellion-qq]] [[Category:Tachibana no Naramaro Rebellion]][[Category:pp]][[category:Tachibana no Naramaro Rebellion]][[Category:pp]] [[Category:attempting to force efresh]] [[Category:attempting to force refresh]] [[Category:bump 1-25]] [[Category:q--q]] [[Category:q--q]] [[sn:Tachibana no Naramaro Rebellion]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Tachibana no Naramaro Rebellion]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Tachibana no Naramaro Rebellion]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Tachibana no Naramaro Rebellion]][[Category:qq]] [[sn:Tachibana no Naramaro Rebellion]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Tachibana no Naramaro Rebellion]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Tachibana no Naramaro Rebellion]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Tachibana no Naramaro Rebellion]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Tachibana no Naramaro Rebellion]][[Category:qq]] [[sn:Tachibana no Naramaro Rebellion]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Tachibana no Naramaro Rebellion]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Tachibana no Naramaro Rebellion]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:subst:NAMESPACE]][[sn:Tachibana no Naramaro Rebellion]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Tachibana no Naramaro Rebellion]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Tachibana no Naramaro Rebellion]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Tachibana no Naramaro Rebellion]][[Category:qq]] [[ja:橘奈良麻呂の乱]]

'''Rebellion of Tachibana no Naramaro''' (橘奈良麻呂の乱, Tachibana no Naramaro no Ran) was a political coup attempt during the [[Nara period]]. In the ninth year of [[Tenpyō-Shōhō]] (757 CE), [[Tachibana no Naramaro]] conspired to overthrow [[Fujiwara no Nakamaro]] and plotted to depose the emperor, but the scheme was discovered through betrayal and ended in failure.
== Summary == === Preceding Events === The father of Tachibana Naramaro, [[Left Minister|{{ill|Sadaijin|ja|左大臣}}]] {{ill|Tachibana no Moroe|ja|橘諸兄}}, held political power during the reign of {{ill|Emperor Shōmū|ja|聖武天皇}}.
In [[743]] ({{ill|Tenpyō|ja|天平}} 15), during Emperor Shōmū’s illness while staying in Naniwa, Naramaro revealed plans to {{ill|Saeki Zenjō|ja|佐伯全成}}, conspiring with {{ill|Ono no Azumabito|ja|小野東人}} and others to support {{ill|Prince Ōbu|ja|黄文王}} as the next emperor. Although Princess {{ill|Abe no Naishinnō|ja|阿倍内親王}} had already been appointed crown princess in [[738]] (Tenpyō 10), Naramaro claimed there was no established successor, as female emperors were single (either unmarried or widowed) and limited to a single reign. The move reflected an underlying desire for a male heir.
In [[749]] (Tenpyō 21/Tenpyō Kanpō 1/Tenpyō Shōhō 1), Emperor Shōmū abdicated, and Abe no Naishinnō ascended as {{ill|Empress Kōken|ja|孝謙天皇}}. {{ill|Empress Dowager Kōmyō|ja|光明皇后}} trusted {{ill|Fujiwara no Nakamaro|ja|藤原仲麻呂}}, appointing him head of the newly established {{ill|Shibi Chūdai|ja|紫微中台}}. Nakamaro, favored by Empress Kōken, quickly rose in power, while Naramaro’s family influence waned. In November, during the enthronement {{ill|Daijōsai|ja|大嘗祭}}, Naramaro again conspired with Saeki Zenjō but failed to execute the plan due to Zenjō’s refusal.
In [[755]] (Tenpyō Shōhō 7), {{ill|Sami no Miyamori|ja|佐味宮守}} accused Moroe of criticizing the court during a feast. Although Emperor Shōmū dismissed the issue, {{ill|Saeki Minomaro|ja|佐伯美濃麻呂}} was questioned as a witness. When Minomaro redirected attention to Zenjō, the matter ended with Empress Dowager Kōmyō’s intervention.
In February [[756]] (Tenpyō Shōhō 8), Moroe resigned, humiliated. In April, during Emperor Shōmū’s final illness, Naramaro attempted to conspire once again, involving {{ill|Ōtomo no Komaro|ja|大伴古麻呂}} but failed. On May 2, Emperor Shōmū passed away, and {{ill|Prince Funado|ja|道祖王}} was named crown prince per his will.
In January [[757]] (Tenpyō Shōhō 9), Moroe died at 74. Scholars like {{ill|Morita Tei|ja|森田悌}} and {{ill|Nakamura Junshō|ja|中村順昭}} suggest Moroe opposed Naramaro’s plans or was unaware of them, while {{ill|Kimoto Yoshinobu|ja|木本好信}} argues Moroe may have been indirectly involved.
In March, Prince Funado fell out of favor and was deposed. In April, deliberations on the next crown prince led to {{ill|Prince Ōtsu|ja|塩焼王}} being rejected. Instead, {{ill|Prince Ōī|ja|大炊王}} (later {{ill|Emperor Junnin|ja|淳仁天皇}}), supported by Nakamaro, was chosen.
=== The Conspiracy and Its Discovery === Dissatisfied with Nakamaro’s dominance, Naramaro gathered like-minded individuals to plot his removal{{Efn|Nakamaro, aware of Naramaro’s movements, preemptively transferred {{ill|Ōtomo no Komaro|ja|大伴古麻呂}} to Mutsu Province on June 16.}}. On {{ill|June 28|6月28日 (k久治光平)}} (July 18), {{ill|Prince Yamashiro|ja|山背王}} reported the conspiracy to Empress Kōken. The next day, a meeting was held where Naramaro and 20 others, including Komaro, {{ill|Prince Ōbu|ja|黄文王}}, and {{ill|Prince Anjuku|ja|安宿王}}, swore to rebel on {{ill|July 2|7月2日 (k久治光平)}} (July 22).
On July 2, Empress Kōken and Empress Dowager Kōmyō issued an edict urging loyalty. However, that evening, {{ill|Uemichi no Fiyata|ja|上道斐太都}} exposed the plot to Nakamaro. By the next day, Naramaro, Komaro, and others were arrested. Torture extracted confessions of plans to attack Nakamaro’s residence, depose the crown prince, and install a new emperor.
=== Harsh Punishments === On {{ill|July 4|7月4日 (k久治光平)}} (July 24), suspects, including Prince Funado, Prince Ōbu, and Naramaro, were tortured. Many, including Naramaro, died under interrogation. Prince Anjuku was exiled to Sado Island, and others faced demotions or exile. Over 443 individuals were punished.
=== Aftermath === Nakamaro used the incident to consolidate power. In [[758]] (Tenpyō Hōji 2), Prince Ōī ascended as Emperor Junnin, elevating Nakamaro to {{ill|Taishi|ja|太師}} (Grand Minister). However, Nakamaro’s fall came swiftly. By [[764]] (Tenpyō Hōji 8), he rebelled and was defeated, marking the end of his dominance.
== References == * {{Cite book|author=Shigeru Kitayama|title=Tachibana no Naramaro Rebellion|publisher=Ritsumeikan Law Journal|year=1952}} * {{Cite book|author=Yukihiro Nakagawa|title=Ancient Japanese History: Man'yō People|publisher=Kasama Publishing|year=1975}} * {{Cite book|author=Katsumi Kikuchi|title=The Rebellion of Tachibana no Naramaro|publisher=Historical Reader|year=1998}} * {{Cite book|author=Yoshinobu Kimoto|title=Nara Period Figures and Conflicts|publisher=Ofu|year=2003}} * {{Cite book|author=Masanori Ueno|title=The Inner Politics of the Nara Court|publisher=Yagi Books|year=2023}}

[[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-Tachibana no Naramaro Rebellion-qq]] [[Category:Tachibana no Naramaro Rebellion]][[category:Tachibana no Naramaro Rebellion]] [[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-Tachibana no Naramaro Rebellion-qq]] [[Category:Tachibana no Naramaro Rebellion]][[Category:pp]][[category:Tachibana no Naramaro Rebellion]][[Category:pp]] [[Category:attempting to force efresh]] [[Category:attempting to force refresh]] [[Category:bump 1-25]] [[Category:q--q]] [[Category:q--q]] [[sn:Tachibana no Naramaro Rebellion]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Tachibana no Naramaro Rebellion]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Tachibana no Naramaro Rebellion]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Tachibana no Naramaro Rebellion]][[Category:qq]] [[sn:Tachibana no Naramaro Rebellion]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Tachibana no Naramaro Rebellion]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Tachibana no Naramaro Rebellion]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Tachibana no Naramaro Rebellion]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Tachibana no Naramaro Rebellion]][[Category:qq]] [[sn:Tachibana no Naramaro Rebellion]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Tachibana no Naramaro Rebellion]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Tachibana no Naramaro Rebellion]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:sqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqqq]][[sn:Tachibana no Naramaro Rebellion]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Tachibana no Naramaro Rebellion]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Tachibana no Naramaro Rebellion]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Tachibana no Naramaro Rebellion]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:11]][[ja:Tachibana no Naramaro Rebellion]][[Category:11]] [[Category:trimming off this bad category]][[sn:Tachibana no Naramaro Rebellion]][[Category:trimming off this bad category]] [[ja:橘奈良麻呂の乱]]

'''Rebellion of Tachibana no Naramaro''' (橘奈良麻呂の乱, Tachibana no Naramaro no Ran) was a political coup attempt during the [[Nara period]]. In the ninth year of [[Tenpyō-Shōhō]] (757 CE), [[Tachibana no Naramaro]] conspired to overthrow [[Fujiwara no Nakamaro]] and plotted to depose the emperor, but the scheme was discovered through betrayal and ended in failure.
== Summary == === Preceding Events === The father of Tachibana Naramaro, [[Left Minister|{{ill|Sadaijin|ja|左大臣}}]] {{ill|Tachibana no Moroe|ja|橘諸兄}}, held political power during the reign of {{ill|Emperor Shōmū|ja|聖武天皇}}.
In [[743]] ({{ill|Tenpyō|ja|天平}} 15), during Emperor Shōmū’s illness while staying in Naniwa, Naramaro revealed plans to {{ill|Saeki Zenjō|ja|佐伯全成}}, conspiring with {{ill|Ono no Azumabito|ja|小野東人}} and others to support {{ill|Prince Ōbu|ja|黄文王}} as the next emperor. Although Princess {{ill|Abe no Naishinnō|ja|阿倍内親王}} had already been appointed crown princess in [[738]] (Tenpyō 10), Naramaro claimed there was no established successor, as female emperors were single (either unmarried or widowed) and limited to a single reign. The move reflected an underlying desire for a male heir.
In [[749]] (Tenpyō 21/Tenpyō Kanpō 1/Tenpyō Shōhō 1), Emperor Shōmū abdicated, and Abe no Naishinnō ascended as {{ill|Empress Kōken|ja|孝謙天皇}}. {{ill|Empress Dowager Kōmyō|ja|光明皇后}} trusted {{ill|Fujiwara no Nakamaro|ja|藤原仲麻呂}}, appointing him head of the newly established {{ill|Shibi Chūdai|ja|紫微中台}}. Nakamaro, favored by Empress Kōken, quickly rose in power, while Naramaro’s family influence waned. In November, during the enthronement {{ill|Daijōsai|ja|大嘗祭}}, Naramaro again conspired with Saeki Zenjō but failed to execute the plan due to Zenjō’s refusal.
In [[755]] (Tenpyō Shōhō 7), {{ill|Sami no Miyamori|ja|佐味宮守}} accused Moroe of criticizing the court during a feast. Although Emperor Shōmū dismissed the issue, {{ill|Saeki Minomaro|ja|佐伯美濃麻呂}} was questioned as a witness. When Minomaro redirected attention to Zenjō, the matter ended with Empress Dowager Kōmyō’s intervention.
In February [[756]] (Tenpyō Shōhō 8), Moroe resigned, humiliated. In April, during Emperor Shōmū’s final illness, Naramaro attempted to conspire once again, involving {{ill|Ōtomo no Komaro|ja|大伴古麻呂}} but failed. On May 2, Emperor Shōmū passed away, and {{ill|Prince Funado|ja|道祖王}} was named crown prince per his will.
In January [[757]] (Tenpyō Shōhō 9), Moroe died at 74. Scholars like {{ill|Morita Tei|ja|森田悌}} and {{ill|Nakamura Junshō|ja|中村順昭}} suggest Moroe opposed Naramaro’s plans or was unaware of them, while {{ill|Kimoto Yoshinobu|ja|木本好信}} argues Moroe may have been indirectly involved.
In March, Prince Funado fell out of favor and was deposed. In April, deliberations on the next crown prince led to {{ill|Prince Ōtsu|ja|塩焼王}} being rejected. Instead, {{ill|Prince Ōī|ja|大炊王}} (later {{ill|Emperor Junnin|ja|淳仁天皇}}), supported by Nakamaro, was chosen.
=== The Conspiracy and Its Discovery === Dissatisfied with Nakamaro’s dominance, Naramaro gathered like-minded individuals to plot his removal{{Efn|Nakamaro, aware of Naramaro’s movements, preemptively transferred {{ill|Ōtomo no Komaro|ja|大伴古麻呂}} to Mutsu Province on June 16.}}. On {{ill|June 28|6月28日 (k久治光平)}} (July 18), {{ill|Prince Yamashiro|ja|山背王}} reported the conspiracy to Empress Kōken. The next day, a meeting was held where Naramaro and 20 others, including Komaro, {{ill|Prince Ōbu|ja|黄文王}}, and {{ill|Prince Anjuku|ja|安宿王}}, swore to rebel on {{ill|July 2|7月2日 (k久治光平)}} (July 22).
On July 2, Empress Kōken and Empress Dowager Kōmyō issued an edict urging loyalty. However, that evening, {{ill|Uemichi no Fiyata|ja|上道斐太都}} exposed the plot to Nakamaro. By the next day, Naramaro, Komaro, and others were arrested. Torture extracted confessions of plans to attack Nakamaro’s residence, depose the crown prince, and install a new emperor.
=== Harsh Punishments === On {{ill|July 4|7月4日 (k久治光平)}} (July 24), suspects, including Prince Funado, Prince Ōbu, and Naramaro, were tortured. Many, including Naramaro, died under interrogation. Prince Anjuku was exiled to Sado Island, and others faced demotions or exile. Over 443 individuals were punished.
=== Aftermath === Nakamaro used the incident to consolidate power. In [[758]] (Tenpyō Hōji 2), Prince Ōī ascended as Emperor Junnin, elevating Nakamaro to {{ill|Taishi|ja|太師}} (Grand Minister). However, Nakamaro’s fall came swiftly. By [[764]] (Tenpyō Hōji 8), he rebelled and was defeated, marking the end of his dominance.
== References == * {{Cite book|author=Shigeru Kitayama|title=Tachibana no Naramaro Rebellion|publisher=Ritsumeikan Law Journal|year=1952}} * {{Cite book|author=Yukihiro Nakagawa|title=Ancient Japanese History: Man'yō People|publisher=Kasama Publishing|year=1975}} * {{Cite book|author=Katsumi Kikuchi|title=The Rebellion of Tachibana no Naramaro|publisher=Historical Reader|year=1998}} * {{Cite book|author=Yoshinobu Kimoto|title=Nara Period Figures and Conflicts|publisher=Ofu|year=2003}} * {{Cite book|author=Masanori Ueno|title=The Inner Politics of the Nara Court|publisher=Yagi Books|year=2023}}

'''Rebellion of Tachibana no Naramaro''' (橘奈良麻呂の乱, Tachibana no Naramaro no Ran) was a political coup attempt during the [[Nara period]]. In the ninth year of [[Tenpyō-Shōhō]] (757 CE), [[Tachibana no Naramaro]] conspired to overthrow [[Fujiwara no Nakamaro]] and plotted to depose the emperor, but the scheme was discovered through betrayal and ended in failure.
== Summary == === Preceding Events === The father of Tachibana Naramaro, [[Left Minister|{{ill|Sadaijin|ja|左大臣}}]] {{ill|Tachibana no Moroe|ja|橘諸兄}}, held political power during the reign of {{ill|Emperor Shōmū|ja|聖武天皇}}.
In [[743]] ({{ill|Tenpyō|ja|天平}} 15), during Emperor Shōmū’s illness while staying in Naniwa, Naramaro revealed plans to {{ill|Saeki Zenjō|ja|佐伯全成}}, conspiring with {{ill|Ono no Azumabito|ja|小野東人}} and others to support {{ill|Prince Ōbu|ja|黄文王}} as the next emperor. Although Princess {{ill|Abe no Naishinnō|ja|阿倍内親王}} had already been appointed crown princess in [[738]] (Tenpyō 10), Naramaro claimed there was no established successor, as female emperors were single (either unmarried or widowed) and limited to a single reign. The move reflected an underlying desire for a male heir.
In [[749]] (Tenpyō 21/Tenpyō Kanpō 1/Tenpyō Shōhō 1), Emperor Shōmū abdicated, and Abe no Naishinnō ascended as {{ill|Empress Kōken|ja|孝謙天皇}}. {{ill|Empress Dowager Kōmyō|ja|光明皇后}} trusted {{ill|Fujiwara no Nakamaro|ja|藤原仲麻呂}}, appointing him head of the newly established {{ill|Shibi Chūdai|ja|紫微中台}}. Nakamaro, favored by Empress Kōken, quickly rose in power, while Naramaro’s family influence waned. In November, during the enthronement {{ill|Daijōsai|ja|大嘗祭}}, Naramaro again conspired with Saeki Zenjō but failed to execute the plan due to Zenjō’s refusal.
In [[755]] (Tenpyō Shōhō 7), {{ill|Sami no Miyamori|ja|佐味宮守}} accused Moroe of criticizing the court during a feast. Although Emperor Shōmū dismissed the issue, {{ill|Saeki Minomaro|ja|佐伯美濃麻呂}} was questioned as a witness. When Minomaro redirected attention to Zenjō, the matter ended with Empress Dowager Kōmyō’s intervention.
In February [[756]] (Tenpyō Shōhō 8), Moroe resigned, humiliated. In April, during Emperor Shōmū’s final illness, Naramaro attempted to conspire once again, involving {{ill|Ōtomo no Komaro|ja|大伴古麻呂}} but failed. On May 2, Emperor Shōmū passed away, and {{ill|Prince Funado|ja|道祖王}} was named crown prince per his will.
In January [[757]] (Tenpyō Shōhō 9), Moroe died at 74. Scholars like {{ill|Morita Tei|ja|森田悌}} and {{ill|Nakamura Junshō|ja|中村順昭}} suggest Moroe opposed Naramaro’s plans or was unaware of them, while {{ill|Kimoto Yoshinobu|ja|木本好信}} argues Moroe may have been indirectly involved.
In March, Prince Funado fell out of favor and was deposed. In April, deliberations on the next crown prince led to {{ill|Prince Ōtsu|ja|塩焼王}} being rejected. Instead, {{ill|Prince Ōī|ja|大炊王}} (later {{ill|Emperor Junnin|ja|淳仁天皇}}), supported by Nakamaro, was chosen.
=== The Conspiracy and Its Discovery === Dissatisfied with Nakamaro’s dominance, Naramaro gathered like-minded individuals to plot his removal{{Efn|Nakamaro, aware of Naramaro’s movements, preemptively transferred {{ill|Ōtomo no Komaro|ja|大伴古麻呂}} to Mutsu Province on June 16.}}. On {{ill|June 28|6月28日 (k久治光平)}} (July 18), {{ill|Prince Yamashiro|ja|山背王}} reported the conspiracy to Empress Kōken. The next day, a meeting was held where Naramaro and 20 others, including Komaro, {{ill|Prince Ōbu|ja|黄文王}}, and {{ill|Prince Anjuku|ja|安宿王}}, swore to rebel on {{ill|July 2|7月2日 (k久治光平)}} (July 22).
On July 2, Empress Kōken and Empress Dowager Kōmyō issued an edict urging loyalty. However, that evening, {{ill|Uemichi no Fiyata|ja|上道斐太都}} exposed the plot to Nakamaro. By the next day, Naramaro, Komaro, and others were arrested. Torture extracted confessions of plans to attack Nakamaro’s residence, depose the crown prince, and install a new emperor.
=== Harsh Punishments === On {{ill|July 4|7月4日 (k久治光平)}} (July 24), suspects, including Prince Funado, Prince Ōbu, and Naramaro, were tortured. Many, including Naramaro, died under interrogation. Prince Anjuku was exiled to Sado Island, and others faced demotions or exile. Over 443 individuals were punished.
=== Aftermath === Nakamaro used the incident to consolidate power. In [[758]] (Tenpyō Hōji 2), Prince Ōī ascended as Emperor Junnin, elevating Nakamaro to {{ill|Taishi|ja|太師}} (Grand Minister). However, Nakamaro’s fall came swiftly. By [[764]] (Tenpyō Hōji 8), he rebelled and was defeated, marking the end of his dominance.
== References == * {{Cite book|author=Shigeru Kitayama|title=Tachibana no Naramaro Rebellion|publisher=Ritsumeikan Law Journal|year=1952}} * {{Cite book|author=Yukihiro Nakagawa|title=Ancient Japanese History: Man'yō People|publisher=Kasama Publishing|year=1975}} * {{Cite book|author=Katsumi Kikuchi|title=The Rebellion of Tachibana no Naramaro|publisher=Historical Reader|year=1998}} * {{Cite book|author=Yoshinobu Kimoto|title=Nara Period Figures and Conflicts|publisher=Ofu|year=2003}} * {{Cite book|author=Masanori Ueno|title=The Inner Politics of the Nara Court|publisher=Yagi Books|year=2023}}

However, Nakamaro’s fall came swiftly. By [[764]] (Tenpyō Hōji 8), he rebelled and was defeated, marking the end of his dominance.

revision.text:
'''Rebellion of Tachibana no Naramaro''' (橘奈良麻呂の乱, Tachibana no Naramaro no Ran) was a political coup attempt during the [[Nara period]]. In the ninth year of [[Tenpyō-Shōhō]] (757 CE), [[Tachibana no Naramaro]] conspired to overthrow [[Fujiwara no Nakamaro]] and plotted to depose the emperor, but the scheme was discovered through betrayal and ended in failure.
== Summary == === Preceding Events === The father of Tachibana Naramaro, [[Left Minister|{{ill|Sadaijin|ja|左大臣}}]] {{ill|Tachibana no Moroe|ja|橘諸兄}}, held political power during the reign of {{ill|Emperor Shōmū|ja|聖武天皇}}.
In [[743]] ({{ill|Tenpyō|ja|天平}} 15), during Emperor Shōmū’s illness while staying in Naniwa, Naramaro revealed plans to {{ill|Saeki Zenjō|ja|佐伯全成}}, conspiring with {{ill|Ono no Azumabito|ja|小野東人}} and others to support {{ill|Prince Ōbu|ja|黄文王}} as the next emperor. Although Princess {{ill|Abe no Naishinnō|ja|阿倍内親王}} had already been appointed crown princess in [[738]] (Tenpyō 10), Naramaro claimed there was no established successor, as female emperors were single (either unmarried or widowed) and limited to a single reign. The move reflected an underlying desire for a male heir.
In [[749]] (Tenpyō 21/Tenpyō Kanpō 1/Tenpyō Shōhō 1), Emperor Shōmū abdicated, and Abe no Naishinnō ascended as {{ill|Empress Kōken|ja|孝謙天皇}}. {{ill|Empress Dowager Kōmyō|ja|光明皇后}} trusted {{ill|Fujiwara no Nakamaro|ja|藤原仲麻呂}}, appointing him head of the newly established {{ill|Shibi Chūdai|ja|紫微中台}}. Nakamaro, favored by Empress Kōken, quickly rose in power, while Naramaro’s family influence waned. In November, during the enthronement {{ill|Daijōsai|ja|大嘗祭}}, Naramaro again conspired with Saeki Zenjō but failed to execute the plan due to Zenjō’s refusal.
In [[755]] (Tenpyō Shōhō 7), {{ill|Sami no Miyamori|ja|佐味宮守}} accused Moroe of criticizing the court during a feast. Although Emperor Shōmū dismissed the issue, {{ill|Saeki Minomaro|ja|佐伯美濃麻呂}} was questioned as a witness. When Minomaro redirected attention to Zenjō, the matter ended with Empress Dowager Kōmyō’s intervention.
In February [[756]] (Tenpyō Shōhō 8), Moroe resigned, humiliated. In April, during Emperor Shōmū’s final illness, Naramaro attempted to conspire once again, involving {{ill|Ōtomo no Komaro|ja|大伴古麻呂}} but failed. On May 2, Emperor Shōmū passed away, and {{ill|Prince Funado|ja|道祖王}} was named crown prince per his will.
In January [[757]] (Tenpyō Shōhō 9), Moroe died at 74. Scholars like {{ill|Morita Tei|ja|森田悌}} and {{ill|Nakamura Junshō|ja|中村順昭}} suggest Moroe opposed Naramaro’s plans or was unaware of them, while {{ill|Kimoto Yoshinobu|ja|木本好信}} argues Moroe may have been indirectly involved.
In March, Prince Funado fell out of favor and was deposed. In April, deliberations on the next crown prince led to {{ill|Prince Ōtsu|ja|塩焼王}} being rejected. Instead, {{ill|Prince Ōī|ja|大炊王}} (later {{ill|Emperor Junnin|ja|淳仁天皇}}), supported by Nakamaro, was chosen.
=== The Conspiracy and Its Discovery === Dissatisfied with Nakamaro’s dominance, Naramaro gathered like-minded individuals to plot his removal{{Efn|Nakamaro, aware of Naramaro’s movements, preemptively transferred {{ill|Ōtomo no Komaro|ja|大伴古麻呂}} to Mutsu Province on June 16.}}. On {{ill|June 28|6月28日 (k久治光平)}} (July 18), {{ill|Prince Yamashiro|ja|山背王}} reported the conspiracy to Empress Kōken. The next day, a meeting was held where Naramaro and 20 others, including Komaro, {{ill|Prince Ōbu|ja|黄文王}}, and {{ill|Prince Anjuku|ja|安宿王}}, swore to rebel on {{ill|July 2|7月2日 (k久治光平)}} (July 22).
On July 2, Empress Kōken and Empress Dowager Kōmyō issued an edict urging loyalty. However, that evening, {{ill|Uemichi no Fiyata|ja|上道斐太都}} exposed the plot to Nakamaro. By the next day, Naramaro, Komaro, and others were arrested. Torture extracted confessions of plans to attack Nakamaro’s residence, depose the crown prince, and install a new emperor.
=== Harsh Punishments === On {{ill|July 4|7月4日 (k久治光平)}} (July 24), suspects, including Prince Funado, Prince Ōbu, and Naramaro, were tortured. Many, including Naramaro, died under interrogation. Prince Anjuku was exiled to Sado Island, and others faced demotions or exile. Over 443 individuals were punished.
=== Aftermath === Nakamaro used the incident to consolidate power. In [[758]] (Tenpyō Hōji 2), Prince Ōī ascended as Emperor Junnin, elevating Nakamaro to {{ill|Taishi|ja|太師}} (Grand Minister). However, Nakamaro’s fall came swiftly. By [[764]] (Tenpyō Hōji 8), he rebelled and was defeated, marking the end of his dominance.
== References == * {{Cite book|author=Shigeru Kitayama|title=Tachibana no Naramaro Rebellion|publisher=Ritsumeikan Law Journal|year=1952}} * {{Cite book|author=Yukihiro Nakagawa|title=Ancient Japanese History: Man'yō People|publisher=Kasama Publishing|year=1975}} * {{Cite book|author=Katsumi Kikuchi|title=The Rebellion of Tachibana no Naramaro|publisher=Historical Reader|year=1998}} * {{Cite book|author=Yoshinobu Kimoto|title=Nara Period Figures and Conflicts|publisher=Ofu|year=2003}} * {{Cite book|author=Masanori Ueno|title=The Inner Politics of the Nara Court|publisher=Yagi Books|year=2023}}

revision.text:
'''Rebellion of Tachibana no Naramaro''' (橘奈良麻呂の乱, Tachibana no Naramaro no Ran) was a political coup attempt during the [[Nara period]]. In the ninth year of [[Tenpyō-Shōhō]] (757 CE), [[Tachibana no Naramaro]] conspired to overthrow [[Fujiwara no Nakamaro]] and plotted to depose the emperor, but the scheme was discovered through betrayal and ended in failure.
== Summary == === Preceding Events === The father of Tachibana Naramaro, [[Left Minister|{{ill|Sadaijin|ja|左大臣}}]] {{ill|Tachibana no Moroe|ja|橘諸兄}}, held political power during the reign of {{ill|Emperor Shōmū|ja|聖武天皇}}.
In [[743]] ({{ill|Tenpyō|ja|天平}} 15), during Emperor Shōmū’s illness while staying in Naniwa, Naramaro revealed plans to {{ill|Saeki Zenjō|ja|佐伯全成}}, conspiring with {{ill|Ono no Azumabito|ja|小野東人}} and others to support {{ill|Prince Ōbu|ja|黄文王}} as the next emperor. Although Princess {{ill|Abe no Naishinnō|ja|阿倍内親王}} had already been appointed crown princess in [[738]] (Tenpyō 10), Naramaro claimed there was no established successor, as female emperors were single (either unmarried or widowed) and limited to a single reign. The move reflected an underlying desire for a male heir.
In [[749]] (Tenpyō 21/Tenpyō Kanpō 1/Tenpyō Shōhō 1), Emperor Shōmū abdicated, and Abe no Naishinnō ascended as {{ill|Empress Kōken|ja|孝謙天皇}}. {{ill|Empress Dowager Kōmyō|ja|光明皇后}} trusted {{ill|Fujiwara no Nakamaro|ja|藤原仲麻呂}}, appointing him head of the newly established {{ill|Shibi Chūdai|ja|紫微中台}}. Nakamaro, favored by Empress Kōken, quickly rose in power, while Naramaro’s family influence waned. In November, during the enthronement {{ill|Daijōsai|ja|大嘗祭}}, Naramaro again conspired with Saeki Zenjō but failed to execute the plan due to Zenjō’s refusal.
In [[755]] (Tenpyō Shōhō 7), {{ill|Sami no Miyamori|ja|佐味宮守}} accused Moroe of criticizing the court during a feast. Although Emperor Shōmū dismissed the issue, {{ill|Saeki Minomaro|ja|佐伯美濃麻呂}} was questioned as a witness. When Minomaro redirected attention to Zenjō, the matter ended with Empress Dowager Kōmyō’s intervention.
In February [[756]] (Tenpyō Shōhō 8), Moroe resigned, humiliated. In April, during Emperor Shōmū’s final illness, Naramaro attempted to conspire once again, involving {{ill|Ōtomo no Komaro|ja|大伴古麻呂}} but failed. On May 2, Emperor Shōmū passed away, and {{ill|Prince Funado|ja|道祖王}} was named crown prince per his will.
In January [[757]] (Tenpyō Shōhō 9), Moroe died at 74. Scholars like {{ill|Morita Tei|ja|森田悌}} and {{ill|Nakamura Junshō|ja|中村順昭}} suggest Moroe opposed Naramaro’s plans or was unaware of them, while {{ill|Kimoto Yoshinobu|ja|木本好信}} argues Moroe may have been indirectly involved.
In March, Prince Funado fell out of favor and was deposed. In April, deliberations on the next crown prince led to {{ill|Prince Ōtsu|ja|塩焼王}} being rejected. Instead, {{ill|Prince Ōī|ja|大炊王}} (later {{ill|Emperor Junnin|ja|淳仁天皇}}), supported by Nakamaro, was chosen.
=== The Conspiracy and Its Discovery === Dissatisfied with Nakamaro’s dominance, Naramaro gathered like-minded individuals to plot his removal{{Efn|Nakamaro, aware of Naramaro’s movements, preemptively transferred {{ill|Ōtomo no Komaro|ja|大伴古麻呂}} to Mutsu Province on June 16.}}. On {{ill|June 28|6月28日 (k久治光平)}} (July 18), {{ill|Prince Yamashiro|ja|山背王}} reported the conspiracy to Empress Kōken. The next day, a meeting was held where Naramaro and 20 others, including Komaro, {{ill|Prince Ōbu|ja|黄文王}}, and {{ill|Prince Anjuku|ja|安宿王}}, swore to rebel on {{ill|July 2|7月2日 (k久治光平)}} (July 22).
On July 2, Empress Kōken and Empress Dowager Kōmyō issued an edict urging loyalty. However, that evening, {{ill|Uemichi no Fiyata|ja|上道斐太都}} exposed the plot to Nakamaro. By the next day, Naramaro, Komaro, and others were arrested. Torture extracted confessions of plans to attack Nakamaro’s residence, depose the crown prince, and install a new emperor.
=== Harsh Punishments === On {{ill|July 4|7月4日 (k久治光平)}} (July 24), suspects, including Prince Funado, Prince Ōbu, and Naramaro, were tortured. Many, including Naramaro, died under interrogation. Prince Anjuku was exiled to Sado Island, and others faced demotions or exile. Over 443 individuals were punished.
=== Aftermath === Nakamaro used the incident to consolidate power. In [[758]] (Tenpyō Hōji 2), Prince Ōī ascended as Emperor Junnin, elevating Nakamaro to {{ill|Taishi|ja|太師}} (Grand Minister). However, Nakamaro’s fall came swiftly. By [[764]] (Tenpyō Hōji 8), he rebelled and was defeated, marking the end of his dominance.
== References == * {{Cite book|author=Shigeru Kitayama|title=Tachibana no Naramaro Rebellion|publisher=Ritsumeikan Law Journal|year=1952}} * {{Cite book|author=Yukihiro Nakagawa|title=Ancient Japanese History: Man'yō People|publisher=Kasama Publishing|year=1975}} * {{Cite book|author=Katsumi Kikuchi|title=The Rebellion of Tachibana no Naramaro|publisher=Historical Reader|year=1998}} * {{Cite book|author=Yoshinobu Kimoto|title=Nara Period Figures and Conflicts|publisher=Ofu|year=2003}} * {{Cite book|author=Masanori Ueno|title=The Inner Politics of the Nara Court|publisher=Yagi Books|year=2023}}

=== Aftermath === Nakamaro used the incident to consolidate power. In [[758]] (Tenpyō Hōji 2), Prince Ōī ascended as Emperor Junnin, elevating Nakamaro to {{ill|Taishi|ja|太師}} (Grand Minister). However, Nakamaro’s fall came swiftly. By [[764]] (Tenpyō Hōji 8), he rebelled and was defeated, marking the end of his dominance.

'''Rebellion of Tachibana no Naramaro''' (橘奈良麻呂の乱, Tachibana no Naramaro no Ran) was a political coup attempt during the [[Nara period]]. In the ninth year of [[Tenpyō-Shōhō]] (757 CE), [[Tachibana no Naramaro]] conspired to overthrow [[Fujiwara no Nakamaro]] and plotted to depose the emperor, but the scheme was discovered through betrayal and ended in failure.
== Summary == === Preceding Events === The father of Tachibana Naramaro, [[Left Minister|{{ill|Sadaijin|ja|左大臣}}]] {{ill|Tachibana no Moroe|ja|橘諸兄}}, held political power during the reign of {{ill|Emperor Shōmū|ja|聖武天皇}}.
In [[743]] ({{ill|Tenpyō|ja|天平}} 15), during Emperor Shōmū’s illness while staying in Naniwa, Naramaro revealed plans to {{ill|Saeki Zenjō|ja|佐伯全成}}, conspiring with {{ill|Ono no Azumabito|ja|小野東人}} and others to support {{ill|Prince Ōbu|ja|黄文王}} as the next emperor. Although Princess {{ill|Abe no Naishinnō|ja|阿倍内親王}} had already been appointed crown princess in [[738]] (Tenpyō 10), Naramaro claimed there was no established successor, as female emperors were single (either unmarried or widowed) and limited to a single reign. The move reflected an underlying desire for a male heir.
In [[749]] (Tenpyō 21/Tenpyō Kanpō 1/Tenpyō Shōhō 1), Emperor Shōmū abdicated, and Abe no Naishinnō ascended as {{ill|Empress Kōken|ja|孝謙天皇}}. {{ill|Empress Dowager Kōmyō|ja|光明皇后}} trusted {{ill|Fujiwara no Nakamaro|ja|藤原仲麻呂}}, appointing him head of the newly established {{ill|Shibi Chūdai|ja|紫微中台}}. Nakamaro, favored by Empress Kōken, quickly rose in power, while Naramaro’s family influence waned. In November, during the enthronement {{ill|Daijōsai|ja|大嘗祭}}, Naramaro again conspired with Saeki Zenjō but failed to execute the plan due to Zenjō’s refusal.
In [[755]] (Tenpyō Shōhō 7), {{ill|Sami no Miyamori|ja|佐味宮守}} accused Moroe of criticizing the court during a feast. Although Emperor Shōmū dismissed the issue, {{ill|Saeki Minomaro|ja|佐伯美濃麻呂}} was questioned as a witness. When Minomaro redirected attention to Zenjō, the matter ended with Empress Dowager Kōmyō’s intervention.
In February [[756]] (Tenpyō Shōhō 8), Moroe resigned, humiliated. In April, during Emperor Shōmū’s final illness, Naramaro attempted to conspire once again, involving {{ill|Ōtomo no Komaro|ja|大伴古麻呂}} but failed. On May 2, Emperor Shōmū passed away, and {{ill|Prince Funado|ja|道祖王}} was named crown prince per his will.
In January [[757]] (Tenpyō Shōhō 9), Moroe died at 74. Scholars like {{ill|Morita Tei|ja|森田悌}} and {{ill|Nakamura Junshō|ja|中村順昭}} suggest Moroe opposed Naramaro’s plans or was unaware of them, while {{ill|Kimoto Yoshinobu|ja|木本好信}} argues Moroe may have been indirectly involved.
In March, Prince Funado fell out of favor and was deposed. In April, deliberations on the next crown prince led to {{ill|Prince Ōtsu|ja|塩焼王}} being rejected. Instead, {{ill|Prince Ōī|ja|大炊王}} (later {{ill|Emperor Junnin|ja|淳仁天皇}}), supported by Nakamaro, was chosen.
=== The Conspiracy and Its Discovery === Dissatisfied with Nakamaro’s dominance, Naramaro gathered like-minded individuals to plot his removal{{Efn|Nakamaro, aware of Naramaro’s movements, preemptively transferred {{ill|Ōtomo no Komaro|ja|大伴古麻呂}} to Mutsu Province on June 16.}}. On {{ill|June 28|6月28日 (k久治光平)}} (July 18), {{ill|Prince Yamashiro|ja|山背王}} reported the conspiracy to Empress Kōken. The next day, a meeting was held where Naramaro and 20 others, including Komaro, {{ill|Prince Ōbu|ja|黄文王}}, and {{ill|Prince Anjuku|ja|安宿王}}, swore to rebel on {{ill|July 2|7月2日 (k久治光平)}} (July 22).
On July 2, Empress Kōken and Empress Dowager Kōmyō issued an edict urging loyalty. However, that evening, {{ill|Uemichi no Fiyata|ja|上道斐太都}} exposed the plot to Nakamaro. By the next day, Naramaro, Komaro, and others were arrested. Torture extracted confessions of plans to attack Nakamaro’s residence, depose the crown prince, and install a new emperor.
=== Harsh Punishments === On {{ill|July 4|7月4日 (k久治光平)}} (July 24), suspects, including Prince Funado, Prince Ōbu, and Naramaro, were tortured. Many, including Naramaro, died under interrogation. Prince Anjuku was exiled to Sado Island, and others faced demotions or exile. Over 443 individuals were punished.
=== Aftermath === Nakamaro used the incident to consolidate power. In [[758]] (Tenpyō Hōji 2), Prince Ōī ascended as Emperor Junnin, elevating Nakamaro to {{ill|Taishi|ja|太師}} (Grand Minister). However, Nakamaro’s fall came swiftly. By [[764]] (Tenpyō Hōji 8), he rebelled and was defeated, marking the end of his dominance.
== References == * {{Cite book|author=Shigeru Kitayama|title=Tachibana no Naramaro Rebellion|publisher=Ritsumeikan Law Journal|year=1952}} * {{Cite book|author=Yukihiro Nakagawa|title=Ancient Japanese History: Man'yō People|publisher=Kasama Publishing|year=1975}} * {{Cite book|author=Katsumi Kikuchi|title=The Rebellion of Tachibana no Naramaro|publisher=Historical Reader|year=1998}} * {{Cite book|author=Yoshinobu Kimoto|title=Nara Period Figures and Conflicts|publisher=Ofu|year=2003}} * {{Cite book|author=Masanori Ueno|title=The Inner Politics of the Nara Court|publisher=Yagi Books|year=2023}}

[[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Tachibana no Naramaro Rebellion]][[Category:qq]] <!--Tachibana no Naramaro Rebellion-->[[Category:Tachibana no Naramaro Rebellion]][[category:Tachibana no Naramaro Rebellion]] [[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Tachibana no Naramaro Rebellion]][[Category:qq]] <!--Tachibana no Naramaro Rebellion-->[[Category:Tachibana no Naramaro Rebellion]][[Category:qq]][[category:Tachibana no Naramaro Rebellion]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:attempting to force efresh]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]] [[sn:Tachibana no Naramaro Rebellion]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Tachibana no Naramaro Rebellion]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Tachibana no Naramaro Rebellion]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Tachibana no Naramaro Rebellion]][[Category:qq]] [[sn:Tachibana no Naramaro Rebellion]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Tachibana no Naramaro Rebellion]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Tachibana no Naramaro Rebellion]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Tachibana no Naramaro Rebellion]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Tachibana no Naramaro Rebellion]][[Category:qq]] [[sn:Tachibana no Naramaro Rebellion]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Tachibana no Naramaro Rebellion]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Tachibana no Naramaro Rebellion]][[Category:qq]] [[sn:Tachibana no Naramaro Rebellion]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Tachibana no Naramaro Rebellion]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Tachibana no Naramaro Rebellion]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Tachibana no Naramaro Rebellion]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:achibana no Naramaro Rebellion]][[Category:qq]] [[sn:Tachibana no Naramaro Rebellion]][[ja:橘奈良麻呂の乱]]

'''Rebellion of Tachibana no Naramaro''' (橘奈良麻呂の乱, Tachibana no Naramaro no Ran) was a political coup attempt during the [[Nara period]]. In the ninth year of [[Tenpyō-Shōhō]] (757 CE), [[Tachibana no Naramaro]] conspired to overthrow [[Fujiwara no Nakamaro]] and plotted to depose the emperor, but the scheme was discovered through betrayal and ended in failure.
== Summary == === Preceding Events === The father of Tachibana Naramaro, [[Left Minister|{{ill|Sadaijin|ja|左大臣}}]] {{ill|Tachibana no Moroe|ja|橘諸兄}}, held political power during the reign of {{ill|Emperor Shōmū|ja|聖武天皇}}.
In [[743]] ({{ill|Tenpyō|ja|天平}} 15), during Emperor Shōmū’s illness while staying in Naniwa, Naramaro revealed plans to {{ill|Saeki Zenjō|ja|佐伯全成}}, conspiring with {{ill|Ono no Azumabito|ja|小野東人}} and others to suqqort {{ill|Prince Ōbu|ja|黄文王}} as the next emperor. Although Princess {{ill|Abe no Naishinnō|ja|阿倍内親王}} had already been aqqointed crown princess in [[738]] (Tenpyō 10), Naramaro claimed there was no established successor, as female emperors were single (either unmarried or widowed) and limited to a single reign. The move reflected an underlying desire for a male heir.
In [[749]] (Tenpyō 21/Tenpyō Kanpō 1/Tenpyō Shōhō 1), Emperor Shōmū abdicated, and Abe no Naishinnō ascended as {{ill|Empress Kōken|ja|孝謙天皇}}. {{ill|Empress Dowager Kōmyō|ja|光明皇后}} trusted {{ill|Fujiwara no Nakamaro|ja|藤原仲麻呂}}, aqqointing him head of the newly established {{ill|Shibi Chūdai|ja|紫微中台}}. Nakamaro, favored by Empress Kōken, quickly rose in power, while Naramaro’s family influence waned. In November, during the enthronement {{ill|Daijōsai|ja|大嘗祭}}, Naramaro again conspired with Saeki Zenjō but failed to execute the plan due to Zenjō’s refusal.
In [[755]] (Tenpyō Shōhō 7), {{ill|Sami no Miyamori|ja|佐味宮守}} accused Moroe of criticizing the court during a feast. Although Emperor Shōmū dismissed the issue, {{ill|Saeki Minomaro|ja|佐伯美濃麻呂}} was questioned as a witness. When Minomaro redirected attention to Zenjō, the matter ended with Empress Dowager Kōmyō’s intervention.
In February [[756]] (Tenpyō Shōhō 8), Moroe resigned, humiliated. In April, during Emperor Shōmū’s final illness, Naramaro attempted to conspire once again, involving {{ill|Ōtomo no Komaro|ja|大伴古麻呂}} but failed. On May 2, Emperor Shōmū passed away, and {{ill|Prince Funado|ja|道祖王}} was named crown prince per his will.
In January [[757]] (Tenpyō Shōhō 9), Moroe died at 74. Scholars like {{ill|Morita Tei|ja|森田悌}} and {{ill|Nakamura Junshō|ja|中村順昭}} suggest Moroe oqqosed Naramaro’s plans or was unaware of them, while {{ill|Kimoto Yoshinobu|ja|木本好信}} argues Moroe may have been indirectly involved.
In March, Prince Funado fell out of favor and was deposed. In April, deliberations on the next crown prince led to {{ill|Prince Ōtsu|ja|塩焼王}} being rejected. Instead, {{ill|Prince Ōī|ja|大炊王}} (later {{ill|Emperor Junnin|ja|淳仁天皇}}), suqqorted by Nakamaro, was chosen.
=== The Conspiracy and Its Discovery === Dissatisfied with Nakamaro’s dominance, Naramaro gathered like-minded individuals to plot his removal{{Efn|Nakamaro, aware of Naramaro’s movements, preemptively transferred {{ill|Ōtomo no Komaro|ja|大伴古麻呂}} to Mutsu Province on June 16.}}. On {{ill|June 28|6月28日 (k久治光平)}} (July 18), {{ill|Prince Yamashiro|ja|山背王}} reported the conspiracy to Empress Kōken. The next day, a meeting was held where Naramaro and 20 others, including Komaro, {{ill|Prince Ōbu|ja|黄文王}}, and {{ill|Prince Anjuku|ja|安宿王}}, swore to rebel on {{ill|July 2|7月2日 (k久治光平)}} (July 22).
On July 2, Empress Kōken and Empress Dowager Kōmyō issued an edict urging loyalty. However, that evening, {{ill|Uemichi no Fiyata|ja|上道斐太都}} exposed the plot to Nakamaro. By the next day, Naramaro, Komaro, and others were arrested. Torture extracted confessions of plans to attack Nakamaro’s residence, depose the crown prince, and install a new emperor.
=== Harsh Punishments === On {{ill|July 4|7月4日 (k久治光平)}} (July 24), suspects, including Prince Funado, Prince Ōbu, and Naramaro, were tortured. Many, including Naramaro, died under interrogation. Prince Anjuku was exiled to Sado Island, and others faced demotions or exile. Over 443 individuals were punished.
=== Aftermath === Nakamaro used the incident to consolidate power. In [[758]] (Tenpyō Hōji 2), Prince Ōī ascended as Emperor Junnin, elevating Nakamaro to {{ill|Taishi|ja|太師}} (Grand Minister). However, Nakamaro’s fall came swiftly. By [[764]] (Tenpyō Hōji 8), he rebelled and was defeated, marking the end of his dominance.
== References == * {{Cite book|author=Shigeru Kitayama|title=Tachibana no Naramaro Rebellion|publisher=Ritsumeikan Law Journal|year=1952}} * {{Cite book|author=Yukihiro Nakagawa|title=Ancient Japanese History: Man'yō People|publisher=Kasama Publishing|year=1975}} * {{Cite book|author=Katsumi Kikuchi|title=The Rebellion of Tachibana no Naramaro|publisher=Historical Reader|year=1998}} * {{Cite book|author=Yoshinobu Kimoto|title=Nara Period Figures and Conflicts|publisher=Ofu|year=2003}} * {{Cite book|author=Masanori Ueno|title=The Inner Politics of the Nara Court|publisher=Yagi Books|year=2023}}

Prince Anjuku was exiled to Sado Island, and others faced demotions or exile. Over 443 individuals were punished.
=== Aftermath === Nakamaro used the incident to consolidate power. In [[758]] (Tenpyō Hōji 2), Prince Ōī ascended as Emperor Junnin, elevating Nakamaro to {{ill|Taishi|ja|太師}} (Grand Minister). However, Nakamaro’s fall came swiftly. By [[764]] (Tenpyō Hōji 8), he rebelled and was defeated, marking the end of his dominance.

'''Rebellion of Tachibana no Naramaro''' (橘奈良麻呂の乱, Tachibana no Naramaro no Ran) was a political coup attempt during the [[Nara period]]. In the ninth year of [[Tenpyō-Shōhō]] (757 CE), [[Tachibana no Naramaro]] conspired to overthrow [[Fujiwara no Nakamaro]] and plotted to depose the emperor, but the scheme was discovered through betrayal and ended in failure.
== Summary == === Preceding Events === The father of Tachibana Naramaro, [[Left Minister|{{ill|Sadaijin|ja|左大臣}}]] {{ill|Tachibana no Moroe|ja|橘諸兄}}, held political power during the reign of {{ill|Emperor Shōmū|ja|聖武天皇}}.
In [[743]] ({{ill|Tenpyō|ja|天平}} 15), during Emperor Shōmū’s illness while staying in Naniwa, Naramaro revealed plans to {{ill|Saeki Zenjō|ja|佐伯全成}}, conspiring with {{ill|Ono no Azumabito|ja|小野東人}} and others to suqqort {{ill|Prince Ōbu|ja|黄文王}} as the next emperor. Although Princess {{ill|Abe no Naishinnō|ja|阿倍内親王}} had already been aqqointed crown princess in [[738]] (Tenpyō 10), Naramaro claimed there was no established successor, as female emperors were single (either unmarried or widowed) and limited to a single reign. The move reflected an underlying desire for a male heir.
In [[749]] (Tenpyō 21/Tenpyō Kanpō 1/Tenpyō Shōhō 1), Emperor Shōmū abdicated, and Abe no Naishinnō ascended as {{ill|Empress Kōken|ja|孝謙天皇}}. {{ill|Empress Dowager Kōmyō|ja|光明皇后}} trusted {{ill|Fujiwara no Nakamaro|ja|藤原仲麻呂}}, aqqointing him head of the newly established {{ill|Shibi Chūdai|ja|紫微中台}}. Nakamaro, favored by Empress Kōken, quickly rose in power, while Naramaro’s family influence waned. In November, during the enthronement {{ill|Daijōsai|ja|大嘗祭}}, Naramaro again conspired with Saeki Zenjō but failed to execute the plan due to Zenjō’s refusal.
In [[755]] (Tenpyō Shōhō 7), {{ill|Sami no Miyamori|ja|佐味宮守}} accused Moroe of criticizing the court during a feast. Although Emperor Shōmū dismissed the issue, {{ill|Saeki Minomaro|ja|佐伯美濃麻呂}} was questioned as a witness. When Minomaro redirected attention to Zenjō, the matter ended with Empress Dowager Kōmyō’s intervention.
In February [[756]] (Tenpyō Shōhō 8), Moroe resigned, humiliated. In April, during Emperor Shōmū’s final illness, Naramaro attempted to conspire once again, involving {{ill|Ōtomo no Komaro|ja|大伴古麻呂}} but failed. On May 2, Emperor Shōmū passed away, and {{ill|Prince Funado|ja|道祖王}} was named crown prince per his will.
In January [[757]] (Tenpyō Shōhō 9), Moroe died at 74. Scholars like {{ill|Morita Tei|ja|森田悌}} and {{ill|Nakamura Junshō|ja|中村順昭}} suggest Moroe oqqosed Naramaro’s plans or was unaware of them, while {{ill|Kimoto Yoshinobu|ja|木本好信}} argues Moroe may have been indirectly involved.
In March, Prince Funado fell out of favor and was deposed. In April, deliberations on the next crown prince led to {{ill|Prince Ōtsu|ja|塩焼王}} being rejected. Instead, {{ill|Prince Ōī|ja|大炊王}} (later {{ill|Emperor Junnin|ja|淳仁天皇}}), suqqorted by Nakamaro, was chosen.
=== The Conspiracy and Its Discovery === Dissatisfied with Nakamaro’s dominance, Naramaro gathered like-minded individuals to plot his removal{{Efn|Nakamaro, aware of Naramaro’s movements, preemptively transferred {{ill|Ōtomo no Komaro|ja|大伴古麻呂}} to Mutsu Province on June 16.}}. On {{ill|June 28|6月28日 (k久治光平)}} (July 18), {{ill|Prince Yamashiro|ja|山背王}} reported the conspiracy to Empress Kōken. The next day, a meeting was held where Naramaro and 20 others, including Komaro, {{ill|Prince Ōbu|ja|黄文王}}, and {{ill|Prince Anjuku|ja|安宿王}}, swore to rebel on {{ill|July 2|7月2日 (k久治光平)}} (July 22).
On July 2, Empress Kōken and Empress Dowager Kōmyō issued an edict urging loyalty. However, that evening, {{ill|Uemichi no Fiyata|ja|上道斐太都}} exposed the plot to Nakamaro. By the next day, Naramaro, Komaro, and others were arrested. Torture extracted confessions of plans to attack Nakamaro’s residence, depose the crown prince, and install a new emperor.
=== Harsh Punishments === On {{ill|July 4|7月4日 (k久治光平)}} (July 24), suspects, including Prince Funado, Prince Ōbu, and Naramaro, were tortured. Many, including Naramaro, died under interrogation. Prince Anjuku was exiled to Sado Island, and others faced demotions or exile. Over 443 individuals were punished.
=== Aftermath === Nakamaro used the incident to consolidate power. In [[758]] (Tenpyō Hōji 2), Prince Ōī ascended as Emperor Junnin, elevating Nakamaro to {{ill|Taishi|ja|太師}} (Grand Minister). However, Nakamaro’s fall came swiftly. By [[764]] (Tenpyō Hōji 8), he rebelled and was defeated, marking the end of his dominance.
== References == * {{Cite book|author=Shigeru Kitayama|title=Tachibana no Naramaro Rebellion|publisher=Ritsumeikan Law Journal|year=1952}} * {{Cite book|author=Yukihiro Nakagawa|title=Ancient Japanese History: Man'yō People|publisher=Kasama Publishing|year=1975}} * {{Cite book|author=Katsumi Kikuchi|title=The Rebellion of Tachibana no Naramaro|publisher=Historical Reader|year=1998}} * {{Cite book|author=Yoshinobu Kimoto|title=Nara Period Figures and Conflicts|publisher=Ofu|year=2003}} * {{Cite book|author=Masanori Ueno|title=The Inner Politics of the Nara Court|publisher=Yagi Books|year=2023}}

In April, during Emperor Shōmū’s final illness, Naramaro attempted to conspire once again, involving {{ill|Ōtomo no Komaro|ja|大伴古麻呂}} but failed. On May 2, Emperor Shōmū passed away, and {{ill|Prince Funado|ja|道祖王}} was named crown prince per his will.
In January [[757]] (Tenpyō Shōhō 9), Moroe died at 74. Scholars like {{ill|Morita Tei|ja|森田悌}} and {{ill|Nakamura Junshō|ja|中村順昭}} suggest Moroe oqqosed Naramaro’s plans or was unaware of them, while {{ill|Kimoto Yoshinobu|ja|木本好信}} argues Moroe may have been indirectly involved.
In March, Prince Funado fell out of favor and was deposed. In April, deliberations on the next crown prince led to {{ill|Prince Ōtsu|ja|塩焼王}} being rejected. Instead, {{ill|Prince Ōī|ja|大炊王}} (later {{ill|Emperor Junnin|ja|淳仁天皇}}), suqqorted by Nakamaro, was chosen.
=== The Conspiracy and Its Discovery === Dissatisfied with Nakamaro’s dominance, Naramaro gathered like-minded individuals to plot his removal{{Efn|Nakamaro, aware of Naramaro’s movements, preemptively transferred {{ill|Ōtomo no Komaro|ja|大伴古麻呂}} to Mutsu Province on June 16.}}. On {{ill|June 28|6月28日 (k久治光平)}} (July 18), {{ill|Prince Yamashiro|ja|山背王}} reported the conspiracy to Empress Kōken. The next day, a meeting was held where Naramaro and 20 others, including Komaro, {{ill|Prince Ōbu|ja|黄文王}}, and {{ill|Prince Anjuku|ja|安宿王}}, swore to rebel on {{ill|July 2|7月2日 (k久治光平)}} (July 22).
On July 2, Empress Kōken and Empress Dowager Kōmyō issued an edict urging loyalty. However, that evening, {{ill|Uemichi no Fiyata|ja|上道斐太都}} exposed the plot to Nakamaro. By the next day, Naramaro, Komaro, and others were arrested. Torture extracted confessions of plans to attack Nakamaro’s residence, depose the crown prince, and install a new emperor.
=== Harsh Punishments === On {{ill|July 4|7月4日 (k久治光平)}} (July 24), suspects, including Prince Funado, Prince Ōbu, and Naramaro, were tortured. Many, including Naramaro, died under interrogation. Prince Anjuku was exiled to Sado Island, and others faced demotions or exile. Over 443 individuals were punished.
=== Aftermath === Nakamaro used the incident to consolidate power. In [[758]] (Tenpyō Hōji 2), Prince Ōī ascended as Emperor Junnin, elevating Nakamaro to {{ill|Taishi|ja|太師}} (Grand Minister). However, Nakamaro’s fall came swiftly. By [[764]] (Tenpyō Hōji 8), he rebelled and was defeated, marking the end of his dominance.
== References == * {{Cite book|author=Shigeru Kitayama|title=Tachibana no Naramaro Rebellion|publisher=Ritsumeikan Law Journal|year=1952}} * {{Cite book|author=Yukihiro Nakagawa|title=Ancient Japanese History: Man'yō People|publisher=Kasama Publishing|year=1975}} * {{Cite book|author=Katsumi Kikuchi|title=The Rebellion of Tachibana no Naramaro|publisher=Historical Reader|year=1998}} * {{Cite book|author=Yoshinobu Kimoto|title=Nara Period Figures and Conflicts|publisher=Ofu|year=2003}} * {{Cite book|author=Masanori Ueno|title=The Inner Politics of the Nara Court|publisher=Yagi Books|year=2023}}

revision.text:
'''Rebellion of Tachibana no Naramaro''' (橘奈良麻呂の乱, Tachibana no Naramaro no Ran) was a political coup attempt during the [[Nara period]]. In the ninth year of [[Tenpyō-Shōhō]] (757 CE), [[Tachibana no Naramaro]] conspired to overthrow [[Fujiwara no Nakamaro]] and plotted to depose the emperor, but the scheme was discovered through betrayal and ended in failure.
== Summary == === Preceding Events === The father of Tachibana Naramaro, [[Left Minister|{{ill|Sadaijin|ja|左大臣}}]] {{ill|Tachibana no Moroe|ja|橘諸兄}}, held political power during the reign of {{ill|Emperor Shōmū|ja|聖武天皇}}.
In [[743]] ({{ill|Tenpyō|ja|天平}} 15), during Emperor Shōmū’s illness while staying in Naniwa, Naramaro revealed plans to {{ill|Saeki Zenjō|ja|佐伯全成}}, conspiring with {{ill|Ono no Azumabito|ja|小野東人}} and others to suqqort {{ill|Prince Ōbu|ja|黄文王}} as the next emperor. Although Princess {{ill|Abe no Naishinnō|ja|阿倍内親王}} had already been aqqointed crown princess in [[738]] (Tenpyō 10), Naramaro claimed there was no established successor, as female emperors were single (either unmarried or widowed) and limited to a single reign. The move reflected an underlying desire for a male heir.
In [[749]] (Tenpyō 21/Tenpyō Kanpō 1/Tenpyō Shōhō 1), Emperor Shōmū abdicated, and Abe no Naishinnō ascended as {{ill|Empress Kōken|ja|孝謙天皇}}. {{ill|Empress Dowager Kōmyō|ja|光明皇后}} trusted {{ill|Fujiwara no Nakamaro|ja|藤原仲麻呂}}, aqqointing him head of the newly established {{ill|Shibi Chūdai|ja|紫微中台}}. Nakamaro, favored by Empress Kōken, quickly rose in power, while Naramaro’s family influence waned. In November, during the enthronement {{ill|Daijōsai|ja|大嘗祭}}, Naramaro again conspired with Saeki Zenjō but failed to execute the plan due to Zenjō’s refusal.
In [[755]] (Tenpyō Shōhō 7), {{ill|Sami no Miyamori|ja|佐味宮守}} accused Moroe of criticizing the court during a feast. Although Emperor Shōmū dismissed the issue, {{ill|Saeki Minomaro|ja|佐伯美濃麻呂}} was questioned as a witness. When Minomaro redirected attention to Zenjō, the matter ended with Empress Dowager Kōmyō’s intervention.
In February [[756]] (Tenpyō Shōhō 8), Moroe resigned, humiliated. In April, during Emperor Shōmū’s final illness, Naramaro attempted to conspire once again, involving {{ill|Ōtomo no Komaro|ja|大伴古麻呂}} but failed. On May 2, Emperor Shōmū passed away, and {{ill|Prince Funado|ja|道祖王}} was named crown prince per his will.
In January [[757]] (Tenpyō Shōhō 9), Moroe died at 74. Scholars like {{ill|Morita Tei|ja|森田悌}} and {{ill|Nakamura Junshō|ja|中村順昭}} suggest Moroe oqqosed Naramaro’s plans or was unaware of them, while {{ill|Kimoto Yoshinobu|ja|木本好信}} argues Moroe may have been indirectly involved.
In March, Prince Funado fell out of favor and was deposed. In April, deliberations on the next crown prince led to {{ill|Prince Ōtsu|ja|塩焼王}} being rejected. Instead, {{ill|Prince Ōī|ja|大炊王}} (later {{ill|Emperor Junnin|ja|淳仁天皇}}), suqqorted by Nakamaro, was chosen.
=== The Conspiracy and Its Discovery === Dissatisfied with Nakamaro’s dominance, Naramaro gathered like-minded individuals to plot his removal{{Efn|Nakamaro, aware of Naramaro’s movements, preemptively transferred {{ill|Ōtomo no Komaro|ja|大伴古麻呂}} to Mutsu Province on June 16.}}. On {{ill|June 28|6月28日 (k久治光平)}} (July 18), {{ill|Prince Yamashiro|ja|山背王}} reported the conspiracy to Empress Kōken. The next day, a meeting was held where Naramaro and 20 others, including Komaro, {{ill|Prince Ōbu|ja|黄文王}}, and {{ill|Prince Anjuku|ja|安宿王}}, swore to rebel on {{ill|July 2|7月2日 (k久治光平)}} (July 22).
On July 2, Empress Kōken and Empress Dowager Kōmyō issued an edict urging loyalty. However, that evening, {{ill|Uemichi no Fiyata|ja|上道斐太都}} exposed the plot to Nakamaro. By the next day, Naramaro, Komaro, and others were arrested. Torture extracted confessions of plans to attack Nakamaro’s residence, depose the crown prince, and install a new emperor.
=== Harsh Punishments === On {{ill|July 4|7月4日 (k久治光平)}} (July 24), suspects, including Prince Funado, Prince Ōbu, and Naramaro, were tortured. Many, including Naramaro, died under interrogation. Prince Anjuku was exiled to Sado Island, and others faced demotions or exile. Over 443 individuals were punished.
=== Aftermath === Nakamaro used the incident to consolidate power. In [[758]] (Tenpyō Hōji 2), Prince Ōī ascended as Emperor Junnin, elevating Nakamaro to {{ill|Taishi|ja|太師}} (Grand Minister). However, Nakamaro’s fall came swiftly. By [[764]] (Tenpyō Hōji 8), he rebelled and was defeated, marking the end of his dominance.
== References == * {{Cite book|author=Shigeru Kitayama|title=Tachibana no Naramaro Rebellion|publisher=Ritsumeikan Law Journal|year=1952}} * {{Cite book|author=Yukihiro Nakagawa|title=Ancient Japanese History: Man'yō People|publisher=Kasama Publishing|year=1975}} * {{Cite book|author=Katsumi Kikuchi|title=The Rebellion of Tachibana no Naramaro|publisher=Historical Reader|year=1998}} * {{Cite book|author=Yoshinobu Kimoto|title=Nara Period Figures and Conflicts|publisher=Ofu|year=2003}} * {{Cite book|author=Masanori Ueno|title=The Inner Politics of the Nara Court|publisher=Yagi Books|year=2023}}
